It would be good to have a test case for this.  One option is to print out the component size for the uninitialized component and then verify that the component size grows when it is initialized.

Patch Set 1: Code-Review-1 Verified-1

I tested this patch out locally.  There was one strange message printed when I wrote to the file to instantiate the second component:

 lfs setstripe -E 16M -c 1 -E 1G -c 3 -E eof -1 /mnt/testfs/test.file
 dd if=/dev/zero of=/mnt/testfs/test.file
 mdt_lvbo_fill()) testfs-MDT0000: expected 368 actual 296.

and again when I instantiated the last component:

 mdt_lvbo_fill()) testfs-MDT0000: expected 464 actual 368.

it appears that the layout was instantiated correctly:

 /mnt/testfs/pfldir/test.file
 composite_header:
  lcm_magic:       0x0BD60BD0
  lcm_size:        464
  lcm_flags:       0
  lcm_layout_gen:  5
  lcm_entry_count: 3
 components:
  - lcme_id:             1
    lcme_flags:          0x10
    lcme_extent.e_start: 0
    lcme_extent.e_end:   16777216
    lcme_offset:         176
    lcme_size:           56
    sub_layout:
      lmm_magic:         0x0BD10BD0
      lmm_seq:           0x200000bd1
      lmm_object_id:     0x1
      lmm_fid:           [0x200000bd1:0x1:0x0]
      lmm_stripe_count:  1
      lmm_stripe_size:   1048576
      lmm_pattern:       1
      lmm_layout_gen:    0
      lmm_stripe_offset: 3
      lmm_objects:
      - 0: { l_ost_idx: 3, l_fid: [0x100030000:0x22:0x0] }

  - lcme_id:             2
    lcme_flags:          0x10
    lcme_extent.e_start: 16777216
    lcme_extent.e_end:   1073741824
    lcme_offset:         232
    lcme_size:           104
    sub_layout:
      lmm_magic:         0x0BD10BD0
      lmm_seq:           0x200000bd1
      lmm_object_id:     0x1
      lmm_fid:           [0x200000bd1:0x1:0x0]
      lmm_stripe_count:  3
      lmm_stripe_size:   1048576
      lmm_pattern:       1
      lmm_layout_gen:    0
      lmm_stripe_offset: 2
      lmm_objects:
      - 0: { l_ost_idx: 2, l_fid: [0x100020000:0x22:0x0] }
      - 1: { l_ost_idx: 1, l_fid: [0x100010000:0x22:0x0] }
      - 2: { l_ost_idx: 0, l_fid: [0x100000000:0x22:0x0] }

  - lcme_id:             3
    lcme_flags:          0x10
    lcme_extent.e_start: 1073741824
    lcme_extent.e_end:   EOF
    lcme_offset:         336
    lcme_size:           128
    sub_layout:
      lmm_magic:         0x0BD10BD0
      lmm_seq:           0x200000bd1
      lmm_object_id:     0x1

      lmm_stripe_count:  4
      lmm_stripe_size:   1048576

      lmm_layout_gen:    0
      lmm_stripe_offset: 0
      lmm_objects:
      - 0: { l_ost_idx: 0, l_fid: [0x100000000:0x23:0x0] }
      - 1: { l_ost_idx: 1, l_fid: [0x100010000:0x23:0x0] }
      - 2: { l_ost_idx: 2, l_fid: [0x100020000:0x23:0x0] }

Tests uploaded by Onyx Autotest from onyx-48vm1 for review-dne-part-1 received by maloo, run on CentOS Linux 7/x86_64: (https://testing.hpdd.intel.com/test_sessions/8376aff8-8e45-45b3-b7b7-9de90b91d995). Ran 7 tests.  1 tests failed: conf-sanity.

Patch Set 9:

All conf-sanity 32a test failure shows that during it's first iteration, it tried to check memory leak on the MDS node, and found 8 bytes leak and fail the test, I've checked all those conf-santy 32a test failure happened here, the exact 8 bytes leak shows in each case.

onyx-48vm7: [17598.570418] LustreError: 11571:0:(class_obd.c:690:obdclass_exit()) obd_memory max: 664699936, leaked: 8
onyx-48vm7: 
onyx-48vm7: Memory leaks detected
 conf-sanity test_32a: @@@@@@ FAIL: Reloading modules 

but during later iterations, the same MDS reload module memory leak check passed, I think it's not related to this patch, and more likely the test picks up some errors left by other tests (meaning there is 8 bytes memory leak lurking in our Lustre code).

Patch Set 9:

Unfortunately, even if the bug is in some other code, we can't land this patch if it is causing conf-sanity to fail. Otherwise we will break all testing on master if this patch was landed.

I guess the other question is why this leak is not hit on a later unmount without this patch applied?  Is it possible that the shrinking is somehow causing the memory allocation accounting to go wrong, which makes it seem like a leak, but it isn't?

You could try running the test with memalloc debug enabled, and then run it through leak-finder.pl to see where the problem is.

parents.subject: LU-6648 wireshark: Process ldlm intent getxattr

commit.subject: LU-9489 lod: keep minimum LOVEA size
commit.message:
LU-9489 lod: keep minimum LOVEA size

For a PFL file, some of its component could be un-instantiated, and
their lov_ost_data_v1 array is not needed, we should keep its LOVEA
as small as possible.

An unstantiated component's stripe offset should be set.

Signed-off-by: Bobi Jam <bobijam.xu@intel.com>
Change-Id: I1cd70399446f063dca4de5b61d7e1dc2c8dde37c
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/27089
Tested-by: Jenkins
Reviewed-by: Niu Yawei <yawei.niu@intel.com>
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
